meta.title: Vector
meta.subject: Vector — a high-performance observability data pipeline

scenes.title: Vector
scenes.footnote: Rust  ·  agent & aggregator  ·  maintained by Datadog

beats.narration: This is Vector -- a high-performance observability data pipeline.
It collects, transforms, and routes all of your logs, metrics, and traces -- from anywhere, to anywhere. Built in Rust, with reliability as its very first design goal.
It is maintained by Datadog's open-source team. We traced it through vector-core and the topology runtime.

nodes.label: Event
nodes.sub: the one type that flows between them

beats.narration: Vector has exactly one shape: a pipeline. Data flows left to right, through three kinds of component.
Sources, where data enters -- forty-six of them: files, sockets, Kafka, cloud APIs, the Kubernetes log stream.
Transforms, in the middle -- eighteen of them -- which filter, aggregate, reshape, and route.
And sinks, where data leaves -- fifty-five destinations, from Elasticsearch to S-3 to any observability vendor you like.
Between every stage flows one thing: an Event. Sources emit Events, transforms rewrite Events, sinks consume them -- one type, end to end.
Observability data comes in three classic shapes -- logs, metrics, and traces. Most tools keep them in three separate pipelines.
Vector does not. It folds all three into a single type -- the Event -- defined once, in the vector-core crate.
A log event is a structured record. A metric is a counter, a gauge, or a histogram. A trace event is a span. Different shapes -- but one enum.
Which means every component -- all forty-six sources, all fifty-five sinks -- speaks exactly one language. The pipeline simply does not care which shape an event is.

scenes.heading: VRL — a language for events
scenes.code:
# A VRL program — runs once per event, in the `remap` transform.
. = parse_json!(.message)            # parse the raw line into fields

.timestamp = parse_timestamp!(.timestamp, "%+")
.env = get_env_var("DEPLOY_ENV") ?? "unknown"

del(.debug)                          # drop a noisy field

if .status >= 500 {
    .severity = "error"
}

beats.narration: The most-used transform is remap -- and it runs a small language all its own: V-R-L, the Vector Remap Language. Purpose-built for exactly one job: reshaping an event.
An event is just data. A VRL program addresses its fields by path -- dot-message, dot-status -- and rewrites them. Here, a raw log line is parsed into structured fields.

VRL takes failure seriously. A function with a bang aborts the program if it fails; the double-question-mark coalesces an error into a fallback. Fallibility lives in the type system, checked before Vector ever starts.
And it mutates and routes -- delete a noisy field, tag an event by severity. VRL is compiled, not interpreted. A bad program is a startup error, never a three A-M surprise.
A Vector config is just components and the wires between them. The topology builder turns that into a running graph -- a directed graph of asynchronous tasks.
Between every connected pair of components sits a buffer -- in memory by default, on disk when you need durability across a restart.

Buffers are also how Vector handles backpressure. If a sink slows down, its buffer fills, and the slowdown propagates upstream -- Vector slows the source, rather than dropping data or exhausting memory.
And end-to-end acknowledgements close the loop. A source holds a record as unacknowledged until the sink confirms it landed -- so a crash mid-pipeline does not silently lose events.
Follow one event the whole way through. A source ingests it and emits it -- but it does not let go. It holds the record as unacknowledged.
The event crosses a buffer into a transform, where a VRL program reshapes it, and continues on toward a sink.
The sink batches events, then flushes the whole batch to its destination -- an Elasticsearch, an S-3 bucket, a vendor's A-P-I.

message.label: batch, then flush to the vendor

beats.narration: Only once that write is confirmed does the sink send an acknowledgement back upstream.
The acknowledgement propagates, hop by hop, all the way back to the source.
And only now does the source mark its record delivered. Crash anywhere in the middle, and the event is simply read again -- never lost, never silently dropped.

scenes.heading: Vector, in four ideas
points: One pipeline — sources in, transforms in the middle, sinks out.
One Event — logs, metrics, and traces, unified into a single type.
VRL — a compiled language built only for reshaping events.
Buffers and acks — backpressure and delivery guarantees, end to end.

beats.narration: So, Vector in four ideas. One pipeline -- sources in, transforms in the middle, sinks out.
One Event type -- logs, metrics, and traces, unified.
V-R-L, a compiled language built for one job -- reshaping events.
And buffers and acknowledgements that give you backpressure and delivery guarantees, end to end. A pipeline you can actually trust with your data.
